title: Thinking Biblically About Life: Parenting
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-17-24 - Thinking Biblically About Life 3 - Parenting - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: What Is Gentle Parenting?
subpoints: Sarah Ockwell-Smith's definition: a holistic philosophy and way of life informed by current science and child psychology
Because it is a worldview, not merely a method, its foundational presuppositions matter
It is organized around seven Cs: Connection, Communication, Control, Containment, Champion, Confidence, Consistency

outline.title: Comparing the Seven Cs: Gentle Parenting vs. Scripture
subpoints: Connection: GP says bad behavior stems from lack of parent connection; Bible says it comes from the heart
Communication: GP says communication is neutral; Bible says our words must build up (Ephesians 4:29)
Control: GP says give children appropriate autonomy; Bible says children are to depend on God and submit to authority
Containment: GP says parents cannot teach children to regulate feelings; Bible says children can learn God's truth for their emotions (Isaiah 41:10)
Champion: GP says always believe children's claims; Bible says the heart is deceitful above all things (Jeremiah 17:9)
Confidence: GP says confidence comes from living in the moment; Bible says confidence comes from abiding in Christ (John 15:1-5)
Consistency: GP says consistent to the other six; Bible says consistent to the Word of God

outline.title: The Core Problem: Emotions at the Center
subpoints: Gentle parenting puts emotions at the center of everything — like trauma-informed counseling puts trauma at the center
If you raise children with emotions at the center, you make them the center — producing the opposite of happiness
The self-esteem movement's first generation proved that self-centeredness does not produce happy, secure people
Gentle parenting is fundamentally anti-authority, which contradicts Scripture's structure for the family

outline.title: Eight Concerns with Gentle Parenting
subpoints: 1. Elevates feelings as supreme
2. Discourages training children to submit to authority
3. Its view of children is incompatible with Scripture (children are foolish and sometimes rebellious)
4. Uses emotions as excuses for sin (which denies the atonement's necessity for those sins)
5. Assumes the inherent goodness of children — contrary to biblical anthropology
6. Teaches that submission to authority is not good for children — contradicts love and obedience as key Christian concepts
7. Celebrates feelings unconditionally without regulating them with biblical truth
8. Denies any value in physical discipline

outline.title: God's Design for Parenting
subpoints: Deuteronomy 6:4-9: a Bible-saturated life — parents teach children, not children teaching parents
Hebrews 12:7-11: chastening is necessary; it yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness
Luke 9:23: how can children learn to take up their cross daily if everything is about them?
The goal is not emotional salvation but the glory of God (2 Corinthians 5:9; 1 Corinthians 10:31)

outline.title: The True Goals of Parenting
subpoints: Happy children: happiness is real but found through loving the Lord and serving Him — not through emotional fulfillment
Confident children: confidence in Christ and growing spiritual maturity — not self-confidence
Secure children: security from trusting the perfect character of God, not from parent-child bonding alone
The goal is children who worship God — and that produces the happiness, confidence, and security gentle parenting promises but cannot deliver

summary: In the third installment of the Thinking Biblically About Life adult Sunday school series, Pastor Tito Lyro critically examines the gentle parenting movement, its chief proponent Sarah Ockwell-Smith, and the biblical alternative. He notes that gentle parenting is not merely a set of practical tips but a worldview organized around seven principles (the seven Cs), all of which rest on foundational presuppositions at odds with Scripture — particularly the assumption of children's inherent goodness and the supremacy of emotions over everything else. By comparing each of the seven Cs to the biblical view, he shows that gentle parenting's understanding of communication, control, containment, authority, and confidence consistently conflicts with what God says. He then identifies eight specific concerns — including the elevation of feelings as supreme, the denial of physical discipline, the assumption of children's inherent goodness, the use of emotions as excuses for sin, and a fundamental resistance to authority — and concludes that the movement cannot produce what it promises. Over against gentle parenting, Pastor Lyro sets two key texts: Deuteronomy 6:4-9 (the Shema's parenting commission: a Bible-saturated, parent-to-child transmission of God's Word) and Hebrews 12:7-11 (the necessity and fruitfulness of chastening). The goal of parenting, he argues, is not emotional salvation but the glory of God — and it is only within that framework that children can find genuine happiness, true confidence, and lasting security.
transcript:
## Introduction

We continue in our series talking about thinking biblically about different subjects common to daily life. And this morning I wanted to think biblically about something called gentle parenting. In the last few years this movement has bubbled up to the surface of parenting methodologies and a lot of Christians have bought into it.

When I say we're going to talk about something that has to do with parenting, some of you might say, okay, it's not for me. But if you're older and past the parenting age, you are still called to disciple younger people. So this is for everyone.

Gentle parenting is not a distinctively Christian way of thinking about parenting. Its main proponent is Sarah Ockwell-Smith. I tried really hard to figure out if she was a Christian and nowhere in her writings was I able to find any claims of Christianity.

This morning I would like for us to understand what gentle parenting is, compare it with the Scriptures, and think of a better way to parent our children for the glory of God. I hope to show you that parenting with gentleness is very different from what gentle parenting teaches.

## What Is Gentle Parenting?

Sarah Ockwell-Smith's definition is: "Gentle parenting is a way of being... it is mindful of current science and child psychology, while remaining respectful of cultural and historical practices of child rearing. It is a holistic philosophy that considers the emotional as well as the practical aspects of parenthood."

Notice that Ockwell-Smith says gentle parenting is not a methodology — it is a way of life. In other words, it is a worldview. When the foundation is rotten, it is really difficult to borrow from the superstructure.

Ockwell-Smith says that gentle parenting is accomplished by following seven Cs: Connection, Communication, Control, Containment, Champion, Confidence, and Consistency.

## Comparing the Seven Cs

**Connection.** Gentle parenting says that a child's bad behavior stems from a lack of connection to the parent. What does the Bible say? Behavior starts from the heart — not from how your parents have related to you.

**Communication.** Gentle parenting says behavior is only communication — expressing physical and emotional needs that must be met — and that communication itself is neutral. So if a child throws a tantrum, that is just neutral communication. What does the Bible say? "Let no corrupt word proceed out of your mouth, but what is good for necessary edification, that it may impart grace to the hearers" (Ephesians 4:29). Communication is not neutral. It has a purpose.

**Control.** Gentle parenting says parents should give appropriate control to children, allowing them to assert autonomy. What they mean is: try to contain behavior and hope for the best; the goal is independent, autonomous children. The Bible says children are to develop dependence on God and submit to parental authority. Gentle parenting is fundamentally anti-authority.

**Containment.** Parents need to contain a child's big emotions because children cannot developmentally regulate feelings. Yet the Bible teaches that children can learn God's truth and bring big feelings to Him for comfort, wisdom, and discernment. Isaiah 41:10 — though addressed to the prophet — is the kind of truth children can trust: "Fear not, for I am with you; be not dismayed, for I am your God."

**Champion.** Gentle parenting says parents must always believe in and believe the claims made by their children. What does the Bible say? "The heart is deceitful above all things" (Jeremiah 17:9). Parents are to sacrifice for their children and teach them wisdom, which includes seeing them as they are.

**Confidence.** Gentle parenting says confidence comes from living in the moment and being surrounded by like-minded people. The Bible says our confidence comes from abiding in Christ (John 15:1-5). We are pilgrims and sojourners going somewhere — the moment is not our ultimate destination.

**Consistency.** Gentle parenting says remaining consistent in practicing the other six leads to learning and understanding. The Bible says consistency to the Word of God leads to learning, growth, and decreased child frustration.

## The Core Problem: Emotions at the Center

Remember how we saw last week that trauma-informed counseling puts trauma at the center of everything? In gentle parenting, what is at the center? Emotions. Everything comes out from emotions. If you respect the emotions, everything else falls into place.

So rather than quick correction of wrong behavior, a gentle parent seeks to understand the underlying emotions driving it. Every interaction is a socio-emotional learning experience.

The claim is that if you do this, you raise happy, confident, and secure children. The problem is that after one full generation of the self-esteem movement in schools, the first generation of adults raised under that philosophy demonstrated they cannot function in the workplace — because real life is not about them. The more we make life about ourselves, the more miserable we become.

Gentle parenting also says spanking should never be used. Let me share what God says.

## God's Design for Parenting

Turn to Deuteronomy 6:4-9 — God's no-testament great commission. As Israel stands on the banks of the Jordan ready to conquer the Promised Land, God says:

"Hear, O Israel: The Lord our God, the Lord is one! You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your strength. And these words which I command you today shall be in your heart. You shall teach them diligently to your children, and shall talk of them when you sit in your house, when you walk by the way, when you lie down, and when you rise up."

Notice that the instruction here is the parents talking to their children — not children talking to their parents. Parents must impart to children the Word of God and the Word of God must saturate the way they live. If what you are doing does not match what you are saying, you are damaging your children. The solution is not to stop saying it — it is to change your behavior.

Now turn to Hebrews 12:7-11:

"If you endure chastening, God deals with you as with sons; for what son is there whom a father does not chasten? But if you are without chastening, of which all have become partakers, then you are illegitimate and not sons. Furthermore, we have had human fathers who corrected us, and we paid them respect. Shall we not much more readily be in subjection to the Father of spirits and live? For they indeed for a few days chastened us as seemed best to them, but He for our profit, that we may be partakers of His holiness. Now no chastening seems to be joyful for the present, but painful; nevertheless, afterward it yields the peaceable fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it."

Chastening — disciplining that hurts — is not cruelty. It is an act of love. And it says clearly: if we don't experience it, we are illegitimate and not sons. Afterward it yields the peaceable fruit of righteousness.

This is 180 degrees from what gentle parenting teaches. And Christian parents must also be pursuing Jesus themselves — confessing sin (1 John 1:9), being careful with their speech (Ephesians 4), not giving into anger (James 1:19-20), loving God (Deuteronomy 6:5), and living self-sacrificially.

In Luke 9:23, Jesus says: "If anyone desires to come after Me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross daily, and follow Me." How are we going to train children to do this by making them the center of life? How can we teach them to pick up their cross daily if all of life is about them and they never have to deny themselves?

## Eight Concerns with Gentle Parenting

1. **Gentle parenting elevates feelings as supreme.** Children need to understand their emotions, and parents must honor their feelings for this to happen.

2. **Gentle parenting discourages training children to submit to authority.** The concept that parents are over their children is to be abandoned. Everyone in the family is an equal.

3. **Gentle parenting's view of children is incompatible with Scripture.** The Bible describes children as foolish, unwise, and sometimes rebellious (Proverbs 22:15). Gentle parenting assumes inherent goodness.

4. **Gentle parenting uses emotions as excuses for sin.** If emotions are ever an excuse for sin, we are saying Christ did not need to die for that sin. The moment we excuse a sin, we deny the need for the atonement to cover it.

5. **Gentle parenting assumes the inherent goodness of children.** If you cater to their emotions, they will desire and pursue what is good. But even the cutest child is corrupted by sin with no inherent good in them.

6. **Gentle parenting teaches that submission to authority is not good for a child.** Yet the relationship between obedience and love is a key component of Christianity: "If you love Me, keep My commandments."

7. **Gentle parenting celebrates children's feelings unconditionally without regulating them with biblical wisdom.** But the Bible tells us to do things we do not feel like doing — "love your enemies" is a command to act contrary to natural feelings. The Bible both commands certain feelings and forbids others.

8. **Gentle parenting denies any value in physical discipline.** God says it is a tool He has given us. Proverbs 22:15: "Foolishness is bound up in the heart of a child; the rod of correction will drive it far from him." Proverbs 23:13-14: "Do not withhold correction from a child... you shall beat him with a rod and deliver his soul from hell." Proverbs 29:15: "The rod and rebuke give wisdom, but a child left to himself brings shame to his mother."

## The True Goals of Parenting

Gentle parenting's goal is happy, confident, and secure children. We want those things too! But they must be understood biblically.

**Happy children:** We want happy children. But Christians know that the goal of finding happiness in oneself is futile. The path to happiness must go through loving the Lord and serving Him. Think of the Beatitudes — happiness is found in poverty of spirit, mourning, meekness, hungering for righteousness, being merciful, pure in heart, making peace. None of these are about meeting your own emotional needs.

**Confident children:** Gentle parenting wants self-confident children whose emotional needs have been met. The Bible wants children growing in maturity — wisdom, convictions, sacrifice, self-control, faithfulness. That is the kind of confidence we are looking for.

**Secure children:** Gentle parenting says security comes from the child's bond with the parent or from emotional security in themselves. Biblical security comes from trust and dependence on the perfect character of God.

The purpose of parenting is not emotional salvation but the glory of God. Paul says in 2 Corinthians 5:9: "We make it our aim, whether present or absent, to be well-pleasing to Him." That is the goal of life — in everything. And 1 Corinthians 10:31: "Whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God."

Psalm 115:1: "Not unto us, O Lord, not unto us, but to Your name give glory, because of Your mercy, because of Your truth." If we are following a chart where the child's emotions are at the center — where the child is at the center — the child is not crying that. Our parenting should reflect this: not unto us, but to God, that glory might go.

The glory of God should shape our parenting. And the goal of Christian parenting is to raise children who worship God. That worship — that orientation toward God rather than self — is what will actually produce the happiness, confidence, and security that gentle parenting promises but cannot deliver.

Let us pray. Father, thank you that you are good. Thank you for your wisdom that comes from your Word. Help us to submit ourselves to the wisdom of your Word in all of life, including the way that we train our children. In Jesus' name. Amen.
